explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} & \frac{\sin \left(\pi \cos ^2 x\right)}{x^2} \\ & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin \left(\pi-\pi \cos ^2 x\right)}{x^2} \\ & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin \left[\pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)\right]}{x^2} \times \frac{\pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)}{\pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)} \\ & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin \left[\pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)\right]}{\pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)} \times \frac{\pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)}{x^2} \end{aligned}$$

at $x \rightarrow 0, \pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)$ tends to zero.

$$\text { So, } \begin{aligned} \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} & \frac{\sin \left[\pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)\right]}{\pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)}=1 \\ & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)}{x^2}=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\pi \sin ^2 x}{x^2} \\ & =\pi \lim _{x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\sin x}{x}\right)^2=\pi(1)^2=\pi \end{aligned}$$

content: $\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{x} \int_0^x(1+\sin 3 t)^{\frac{1}{t}} d t$ is equal to
explanation:
$\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\int_0^x(1+\sin 3 t)^{\frac{1}{t}} d t}{x}$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Differentiating numerator and denominator }\\ &L=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{(1+\sin 3 x)^{\frac{1}{x}}}{1} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & L=e^{\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{x} \sin 3 x} \Rightarrow L=e^{\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{3 \sin 3 x}{3 x}} \\ \Rightarrow & L=e^{3 \times 1}=e^3 \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { } \because f(x)=|\log 2-\sin x| \text { and } g(x)=f(f(x)) \\ & x \in R \\ & \text { For } x \rightarrow 0, \log 2>\log x \\ & \therefore \quad f(x)=\log 2-\sin x \\ & \text { and } \quad g(x)=\log 2-\sin (f(x)) \\ & =\log 2-\sin (\log 2-\sin x) \\ \end{aligned}$$

Clearly, $$g(x)$$ is differentiable at $$x=0$$ as $$\sin x$$ is differentiable.

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Now, } g^{\prime}(x) & =-\cos (\log 2-\sin x)(-\cos x) \\ & =\cos x \cdot \cos (\log 2-\sin x) \\ \Rightarrow \quad g^{\prime}(0) & =1 \cdot \cos (\log 2)=\cos \log (2) \end{aligned}$$

content: The value of $$\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \infty}\left[\frac{p^{1 / x}+q^{1 / x}+r^{1 / x}+s^{1 / x}}{4}\right]^{3 x}, p, q, r, s>0 \text {, }$$ is
explanation:
Given, $$\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \infty}\left[\frac{p^{1 / x}+q^{1 / x}+r^{1 / x}+s^{1 / x}}{4}\right]^{3 x}, p, q,r, s>0$$

Let us put $$\frac{1}{x}=z$$ so that $$x \rightarrow \infty \Rightarrow z \rightarrow 0$$

$$\therefore \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \infty}\left[\frac{p^{1 / x}+q^{1 / x}+r^{1 / x}+s^{1 / x}}{4}\right]^{3 x}$$

$$=\lim _\limits{z \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{p^z+q^z+r^z+s^z}{4}\right]^{\frac{3}{z}}$$, which is the form of $$1^{\infty}$$.

$$\begin{aligned} & =e^{\lim _\limits{z \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{p^z+q^z+r^z+s^z}{4}-1\right] 3 / z} \\ & =e^{\lim _\limits{z \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{p^z-1}{z}+\frac{q^z-1}{z}+\frac{r^z-1}{z}+\frac{s^z-1}{z}\right] 3 / 4} \\ & =e^{\frac{3}{4}\left(\log e^p+\log e^q+\log e^r+\log e^s\right)} \\ & =e^{\frac{3}{4} \log _e(p q r s)}=(p q r s)^{\frac{3}{4}} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}(\sin x+\cos x)^{\operatorname{cosec} x} & ,-\frac{\pi}{2}< x<0 \\ a & ,x=0 \\ \frac{e^{1 / x}+e^{2 / x}+e^{3 / x}}{a e^{-2+\frac{1}{x}}+b e^{-1+\frac{3}{x}}} & , 0< x<\frac{\pi}{2}\end{array}\right.$$ is continuous at $$x=0$$, then the value of $$(b, a)$$ is
explanation:
We have, $$f(0)=a$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}[\sin (-h)+\cos (-h)]^{\operatorname{cosec}(-h)} \\ & =\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}[\cos h-\sin h]^{-\operatorname{cosec} h} \end{aligned}$$

$$ = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} {[1 + \cosh - \sinh - 1]^{\left[ {{1 \over {\cosh - \sinh - 1}} \times {{\cosh - \sinh - 1} \over { - \sinh }}} \right]}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} {[1 + (\cosh - \sinh - 1)]^{\left[ {{1 \over {\cosh - \sinh - 1}}} \right]\left[ {{{1 + \sinh - \cosh } \over {\sinh }}} \right]}}$$

$$ = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} [1 + (\cosh - \sinh - 1)]$$

$$ = {e^{\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} }}\left[ {{{\sin {h \over 2} + \cos {h \over 2}} \over {\cos {h \over 2}}}} \right]\quad$$ $$\left[\because \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0}(1+x)^{\frac{1}{x}}=e\right]$$

$$=e$$

$$\text { and } \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{\frac{1}{h}}+e^{\frac{2}{h}}+e^{\frac{3}{h}}}{a e^{-2+\frac{1}{h}}+b e^{-1+\left(\frac{3}{h}\right)}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{\frac{2}{h}}+e^{-\frac{1}{h}}+1}{\left(a e^{-2}\right) e^{-\frac{2}{h}}+\left(b e^{-1}\right)} \\ & =\frac{0+0+1}{\left(a e^{-2}\right) 0+b e^{-1}}=\frac{e}{b} \end{aligned}$$

For the function to be continuous at $$x=0$$, we have

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=f(0)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x) \\ \Rightarrow \quad & e=a=\frac{e}{b} \Rightarrow a=e \text { and } b=1 \end{array}$$

Hence, $$(1, e)$$

If we put

$$\begin{aligned} x & =2, f(z)=\frac{(2)^3-3(2)^2+4}{(2)^4-7(2)-2} \\ & =\frac{8-12+4}{16-14-2}=\frac{0}{0} \end{aligned}$$

$$\left(\frac{0}{0}\right) \text { form, use L'Hospital rule, }$$

$$f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2} \frac{\frac{d}{d x}\left(x^3-3 x^2+4\right)}{\frac{d}{d x}\left(x^4-7 x-2\right)}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 2} \frac{3 x^2-6 x+0}{4 x^3-7-0} \\ & f(2)=\frac{3(2)^2-6(2)}{4(2)^3-7} \\ & f(2)=\frac{12-12}{32-7}=0 \end{aligned}$$

Hence, $$\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2} \frac{x^3-3 x^2+4}{x^4-7 x-2}=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0}\left\{\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+x\right)\right\}^{1 / x}=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0}\left\{\frac{1+\tan x}{1-\tan x}\right\}^{1 / x} \\ & =\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0}\left\{1+\left(\frac{1+\tan x}{1-\tan x}-1\right)\right\}^{1 / x} \\ & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0}\left\{1+\frac{2 \tan x}{1-\tan x}\right\}^{1 / x} \quad\left(\text { form } 1^{\infty}\right) \\ & =e^{\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \tan x}{1-\tan x} \cdot \frac{1}{x}} \\ & =e^{2 \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\tan x}{x}\right) \cdot \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{1-\tan x}} \\ & =e^{2 \cdot 1 \cdot\left(\frac{1}{1-0}\right)}=e^2 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}\frac{(1-\cos 4 x)}{x^2}, & \text { if } x < 0 \\ a, & \text { if } x=0, \\ \frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{(16+\sqrt{x})}-4}, & \text { if } x > 0\end{array}\right.$$ then $$f(x)$$ is continuous at $$x=0$$, for $$a$$
explanation:
Given, $$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}\frac{(1-\cos 4 x)}{x^2}, & \text { if } x < 0 \\ a, & \text { if } x=0, \\ \frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{(16+\sqrt{x})}-4}, & \text { if } x > 0\end{array}\right.$$

$$\mathrm{LHL}=f(0-0)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} f(0-h)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{1-\cos 4(0-h)}{(0-h)^2} \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left\{\frac{(-4 h)^2}{2 !}-\frac{(-4 h)^4}{4 !}+\ldots\right\}}{(-h)^2}\end{aligned}$$

$$=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}\left\{\frac{\frac{16 h^2}{2 !}-\frac{(4 h)^4}{4 !}+\ldots}{h^2}\right\}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{16}{2 !}-\frac{4^4 h^2}{4 !}+\ldots \\ & =\frac{16}{2}=8\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { RHL }=f(0+0) & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} f(0+h) \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sqrt{h}}{\sqrt{16+\sqrt{h}}-4} \quad\left(\frac{0}{0} \text { form }\right) \end{aligned}$$

Using 'L' Hospital rule,

$$\begin{aligned} & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{h}} \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{16+\sqrt{h}} \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{h}}} \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} 2 \sqrt{16+\sqrt{h}}=2 \sqrt{16+0} \\ & =2 \times 4=8 \end{aligned}$$

Since, $$f(x)$$ is continuous at $$x=0$$.

$$\begin{array}{lc} \therefore & \mathrm{LHL}=\mathrm{RHL}=f(0) \\ \Rightarrow & \mathrm{a}=8 \end{array}$$
